topic: Someone receives flowers with no card attached
raw_output:
[story 1]

The delivery person, a young man with a bright smile, placed the bouquet on the entryway table. Crimson roses, impossibly perfect, and sprays of white lilies. I stood frozen in the hallway, my breath catching somewhere in my chest. My hands, which had been gripping the doorframe so tightly my knuckles were white, slowly unclenched. I stared at the arrangement, the vibrant colors seeming too loud, too demanding. There was no note, no signature, just the heavy, silent presence of the flowers. A tremor ran through my legs. Who was this? Why? I took a hesitant step forward, then stopped, my gaze darting towards the door, half-expecting someone to materialize and demand an explanation. My throat felt suddenly constricted, making it difficult to even draw a steady breath.

[story 2]

I found the box tucked beside my laptop, the paper slightly crushed. Inside, the flowers were opulent—deep purple irises and delicate blush peonies. My fingers traced the smooth, cool petals, a strange flutter starting deep in my stomach. Every nerve ending seemed hyper-aware, cataloging every shadow in the room. I kept glancing at the clock on the wall, the second hand ticking with exaggerated slowness. I picked up a stray leaf, turning it over and over in my palm, seeking some anchor in the sudden swirling uncertainty. My jaw tightened, and I swallowed hard, the dryness in my mouth making the simple act of breathing feel like a monumental effort. I needed to know what this meant, but the silence of the apartment amplified every internal question into a deafening roar.

[story 3]

The package arrived just as I was trying to focus on sorting through bills. When I lifted the lid, the scent of the blooms—sweet, heady, almost suffocating—hit me immediately. Yellow sunflowers, bright and unapologetic. I set the box down on the kitchen counter, my entire body tensing up. I walked to the window, pulling the curtain aside just a crack, peering out at the busy street below, trying to ground myself in the mundane reality of traffic and hurried pedestrians. My heart began a frantic drumming against my ribs. I paced three steps back and forth across the linoleum floor, my heels clicking sharply. I kept re-reading the empty space where a card should have been, my eyes scanning the room as if expecting a hidden message to appear on the wallpaper.
